summary: DeVault argues that federation represents the best architectural model for building internet-scale communication systems that resist centralization. He positions federation as a middle ground between centralized and peer-to-peer systems, enjoying the advantages of both while avoiding their drawbacks. Federated systems distribute governance and costs across many independent server operators, making them sustainable without corporate backing. He emphasizes that federation enables non-technical users to participate without the burden of running their own infrastructure, unlike P2P systems. DeVault concludes that the free software community must champion federation because corporations have no incentive to build systems that empower users over profits.
key_points: Federation occupies a middle ground between centralized and P2P architectures, combining the advantages of both with few drawbacks
Distributed governance lets users choose instances with rules they agree with, while operators decide who they federate with
Federation's scaling costs are distributed among many small operators, often cheap enough to run out of pocket or fund through modest donations
Unlike P2P systems, federation lets technical volunteers extend access to non-technical users without burdening them with server maintenance
Corporations will never champion federation because centralization is more profitable — the free software community must drive it
money_quotes: The key trait of a software system which is federated is that the servers are controlled by independent, sovereign entities, and that they exist together under a common web of communication protocols and social agreements.

This system also makes it hard for marketing and spam to get a foothold — it optimizes for a self-governing system of human beings talking to human beings, and not for corporations to push their products.
The free software community needs to rally behind federation, because no one else will.
They would much rather build walled gardens and centralize, centralize, centralize — it's more profitable!
Democratic software which puts control into the hands of the users is something we're going to have to take for ourselves.
Cryptocurrency is the digitally woke techbro's ponzi scheme, and is a massive waste of electricity and developer effort.

Unlike P2P systems, the federated model allows volunteer sysadmins to use their skills to expand access to the service to non-technical users, without placing the burden on those non-technical users to set up, understand, maintain, or secure servers or esoteric software.
key_insight: Federation is the only architecture that distributes both governance and cost without requiring technical sophistication from end users, but it will only succeed if the free software community champions it — corporations never will.
